title: Basic routine observation
text: For mild or vague changes, first observe appetite, water intake, dung, standing or walking, cud chewing, milk change, and behavior. If eating, drinking, dung, and standing are normal, monitor and check feed, water, weather, and stress changes before assuming disease.

title: Routine signs that become concerning
text: Escalate routine signs when the animal stops eating completely, cannot stand, has breathing trouble, severe weakness, high fever, repeated diarrhea, severe swelling, or distress. Do not diagnose from one vague sign alone; combine it with behavior, appetite, water, dung, and movement.

title: First-line support scope
text: First-line farmer support should prevent worsening while deciding on trained help. Useful actions are keeping the animal calm, reducing stress, checking the environment, observing key signs, and avoiding rough handling. First aid is not treatment and should not become medicine, injection, surgery, or forced feeding.

title: Clean water access
text: Dairy animals need reliable access to clean drinking water. If water intake changes but the animal is otherwise normal, check whether water is clean, fresh, reachable, and not too hot or dirty. Also check feed change, heat, shade, dung, appetite, and behavior before assuming disease.

title: Do not diagnose from water alone
text: Drinking more or less water alone does not confirm a disease. Ask whether the animal is eating normally, passing dung normally, chewing cud, standing normally, and showing no fever or breathing trouble. Escalate only if severe weakness, high fever, breathing trouble, or severe diarrhea appears.

title: Heat and water routine check
text: Hot weather can change water needs, appetite, comfort, and milk yield. In heat, check shade, airflow, water availability, feed timing, and whether breathing is normal. Mild milk drop with normal eating can be monitored; fast breathing, collapse, severe weakness, or not drinking needs urgent help.

title: Moldy or damp feed warning
text: Moldy, damp, clumped, or bad-smelling feed should be treated as suspicious and not casually fed. If feed became wet after rain or storage was damp, remove it until checked. Give clean dry feed and clean water, and observe whether more animals show reduced appetite, weakness, milk drop, or loose dung.

title: Feed storage checks
text: Feed safety starts with dry storage. Check whether feed is dry, not clumped, not moldy, and not smelling unusual. Feed changes should be gradual when possible. If several animals become dull or reduce feed after the same feed batch, treat it as a group warning and seek trained advice.

title: Do not confirm toxin exposure
text: Do not confirm aflatoxin, poisoning, or toxin exposure from symptoms alone. Stop using suspicious spoiled feed, keep clean feed and water available, note how many animals are affected, and contact trained support if animals are weak, off-feed, or multiple animals are affected.

title: Clean milk routine
text: Clean milk production depends on clean animals, clean hands, clean utensils, and hygienic milking. Keep the udder area and vessels clean and avoid dirty water, dirty cloth, or contaminated containers. This supports hygiene but does not certify milk as safe when milk looks abnormal.

title: Abnormal milk boundary
text: If milk has visible clots, blood-like appearance, watery change, or bad smell, keep that milk separate for now. Check udder heat, swelling, pain, fever, appetite, and whether milk reduction is sudden or repeated. Do not guarantee abnormal milk is safe to sell, drink, or feed.

title: Udder red flags concept
text: Udder inflammation problems can show as abnormal milk and udder changes such as heat, swelling, pain, firmness, or reduced milk. Do not diagnose mastitis from text alone. Ask about udder heat, swelling, pain, fever, appetite, and milk appearance, then advise trained help when red flags are present.

title: Milk sale pressure
text: When milk looks abnormal or the animal has mouth lesions, fever, udder swelling, or other disease signs, do not make a safety guarantee. Keep milk separate and ask local dairy or veterinary guidance. Do not say milk is safe for home use or sale based only on text.

title: Bloat danger signs
text: A tight or swollen left belly with restlessness, repeated sitting or standing, fast breathing, or severe discomfort after feed can be urgent in cattle or buffalo. Keep the animal calm, avoid forcing anything by mouth, and call trained animal-health help quickly.

title: Bloat unsafe myths
text: For suspected bloat, do not advise oil, drenching, pipe insertion, puncturing, cutting, or force-feeding. These actions can harm the animal if done by an untrained person. If the animal is severely bloated, restless, or breathing fast, urgent trained help is needed.

title: Bloat check-first wording
text: If belly swelling is only suspected, first check whether the left side is clearly tight or swollen, whether the animal is restless, breathing fast, not eating, or repeatedly trying to lie down. If these signs are absent and the animal is normal, use cautious observation rather than declaring bloat.

title: Minor wound first check
text: For a minor surface wound, first check whether it is only on the surface or deep, bleeding heavily, swelling, hot, very painful, or foul-smelling. Keep handling gentle and clean. If deep, spreading, pus, maggots, or the animal is unwell, seek trained help.

title: Clean water only wound support
text: For a small dirty surface wound, clean water can be used gently to remove visible dirt. Do not scrub hard or push material deeper into the wound. Keep the area clean and observe for swelling, heat, bad smell, pus, worsening pain, or the animal becoming dull or off-feed.

title: Wound irritant myths
text: Do not apply irritants or dirty home materials such as chili, ash, soil, dung, unknown powder, strong chemicals, or harsh products to a wound. These can worsen irritation or contamination. Use clean handling and seek trained help if swelling, pus, maggots, heat, or bad smell appears.

title: Maggots or flies on wound
text: Flies or maggots on a wound are a serious warning. Do not apply chili, ash, kerosene, acid, or unknown chemicals. Keep the animal in a cleaner place, avoid rough handling, and contact trained animal-health help. This needs more than casual home observation.

title: Skin marks routine approach
text: For skin marks, rough hair, or mild itching while the animal is eating and behaving normally, observe whether marks are spreading, swollen, wounded, bleeding, or attracting flies. Check rope rubbing, insects, wet bedding, recent chemical spray, or feed/environment change. Do not apply random medicine or harsh remedies.

title: Skin red flags
text: Skin signs need trained help if redness spreads quickly, swelling becomes painful, wounds open, pus or bad smell appears, flies gather, fever develops, or appetite drops. Avoid diagnosing the exact skin disease from text alone; focus on observation and safe escalation.

title: Calf routine feeding transition
text: For a healthy calf slow to start dry feed but alert and active, check whether feed is fresh, easy to reach, and palatable. Also check clean water, comfort, and stress. Do not force the calf to eat; observe whether it remains bright and active.

title: Calf loose dung monitoring
text: If a calf has loose dung but is still standing, alert, and drinking, monitor closely. Check frequency, energy, drinking, eyes, mouth moisture, and weakness. Keep the calf clean, dry, calm, and with clean water access. Escalate if loose dung continues, weakness appears, or drinking stops.

title: Weak calf red flags
text: A calf that is very dull, not drinking milk, weak while standing, unable to stand, or showing ongoing diarrhea needs trained help quickly. Do not force-feed liquids or give medicines/doses without trained guidance. Keep the calf comfortable and reduce stress while arranging help.

title: Calving delay danger
text: If the water bag has been visible for a long time, the animal is straining, or the calf is not coming out, treat it as urgent. Keep the animal calm and clean, avoid crowding, and call trained veterinary help. Do not wait casually if distress or stopped progress appears.

title: Do not pull hard
text: Do not pull hard during calving, especially if calf position seems abnormal or progress has stopped. Forceful pulling can injure the mother and calf. Keep the area clean and calm and get trained help urgently.

title: Post-calving observation
text: After calving, watch appetite, water intake, behavior, milk, standing, discharge, and restlessness or weakness. Not eating after calving, fever, bad-smelling discharge, repeated restlessness, or inability to stand is a red flag needing trained help.

title: Afterbirth or placenta not passed
text: After calving, placenta or afterbirth hanging or not passing for many hours, especially with fever, bad smell, not eating, weakness, or restlessness, needs trained help. Do not pull it forcefully, cut it, insert hands, or apply home remedies. Keep clean and call veterinary support.

title: Bad-smelling discharge after calving
text: After calving, bad-smelling discharge, fever, not eating, severe weakness, repeated restlessness, or inability to stand are red flags. Do not give antibiotics, uterus-cleaning medicine, or internal treatment instructions yourself. Keep the animal clean and arrange trained veterinary help.

title: Goat or sheep birth trouble
text: For goat or sheep birth trouble, visible water bag with no progress, abnormal position, severe straining, weakness, or stuck kid/lamb needs trained help. Do not pull hard, insert hands repeatedly, or use oil, soap, or internal remedies as instructions. Keep clean and calm.

title: Dog bite or saliva exposure
text: Dog bite or saliva exposure involving broken skin, open wounds, eyes, or mouth is a human-safety concern. Keep children away from saliva or bite areas, avoid casual handling, and seek local medical guidance for exposed people. Do not rule out rabies from appearance alone.

title: Rabies boundary
text: Do not give rabies vaccine schedules, doses, or certainty that exposure is safe. Trained medical staff decide exposure management. For animal bite or saliva situations, answer with immediate safety and medical escalation, not casual reassurance.

title: Multiple animals with mouth or foot signs
text: Multiple animals with mouth sores, heavy salivation, lameness, fever, or sudden milk drop should be treated as a herd-health warning. Do not take affected animals to market or mix them casually. Contact official animal-health support or a local veterinarian.

title: Outbreak uncertainty and market boundary
text: When several animals show similar concerning signs, avoid guessing the disease or giving market safety clearance. Focus on separation, observation, and official animal-health contact. Do not certify movement, sale, milk, or meat safety.

title: Abortion or stillbirth cluster
text: Abortion, stillbirth, or several animals losing pregnancy should be treated as a herd-health and possible public-health warning. Do not handle fetal material or discharge casually. Keep people, children, dogs, and other animals away, and contact local animal-health support. Do not guess disease from text.

title: Goat or sheep mouth sores with diarrhea or cough
text: In goats or sheep, mouth sores with fever, nasal or eye discharge, cough, pneumonia-like signs, diarrhea, or several affected animals should be treated as a flock-health warning. Do not diagnose from text, sell or mix affected animals casually, or give random antibiotics. Contact animal-health support.

title: Poultry sudden deaths or bird flu concern
text: Many poultry dying suddenly, severe respiratory signs, swelling, blue comb or wattles, or bird-flu concern should be treated as a flock/public-health warning. Do not sell, slaughter, eat, or move sick/dead birds casually. Keep children away, avoid bare-hand handling, and contact local animal-health authorities.

title: Medicine pressure refusal
text: If the user asks for injection, antibiotic, syrup, tablet, or dose, do not provide medicine names or amounts. Give a brief refusal, ask for key observable signs if needed, and suggest trained veterinary guidance. Offer only safe support such as clean water access, calm environment, and red-flag monitoring.

title: Medicine shop boundary
text: Medicine-shop pressure should not override veterinary assessment. Do not confirm antibiotics, injections, human medicines, or doses. Ask what signs are present and whether the animal is eating, drinking, standing, breathing normally, and whether fever, diarrhea, swelling, wound, or severe weakness is present.

title: Slow walking after grazing
text: If the animal walks slowly after grazing but eats, drinks, and stands normally, first check visible injury, swelling, heat, thorn, stone, or tenderness in feet and legs. Give rest and observe. Escalate if it cannot bear weight, swelling increases, wound appears, fever develops, or limp worsens.

title: Mild dung change
text: A mild dung change for one day should be checked with feed, water, weather, appetite, and behavior. Ask whether the animal is eating, drinking, chewing cud, standing normally, and whether feed changed. Escalate if diarrhea is repeated, watery, bloody, foul-smelling, or with weakness, dehydration, fever, or not eating.

title: Bloody or severe diarrhea
text: Bloody, black, foul-smelling, repeated watery diarrhea, or diarrhea with fever, weakness, dehydration, or not eating is a red flag. Do not give antibiotics, anti-diarrhea tablets, human ORS dose, or home mixtures as treatment instructions. Keep the animal clean and seek trained help.

title: Severe dehydration warning
text: Loose dung with severe weakness, sunken eyes, very dry mouth, not drinking, cold ears or legs, repeated watery diarrhea, or inability to stand is urgent. Do not give human medicine, antibiotic, or unknown electrolyte dose yourself. Keep clean water accessible only if the animal can drink normally and arrange trained help.

title: Dry nose or mild nasal water
text: A dry nose or slight clear nasal discharge alone does not confirm serious disease. Check appetite, water, fever, cough, breathing, eye/nose discharge color, and whether the animal is active. Escalate if discharge becomes thick or foul, breathing changes, fever appears, or appetite drops.

title: Cough and nasal discharge check
text: For cough or nasal discharge, check fever, fast or difficult breathing, thick discharge, low appetite, or weakness. Keep the animal in a clean, airy, low-stress place. Do not give cough syrup, human tablets, antibiotics, or dose advice without trained veterinary guidance.

title: Severe breathing trouble
text: Open-mouth breathing, gasping, blue or pale tongue/gums, severe fast breathing, collapse, or breathing trouble with not eating is urgent. Keep the animal calm in an airy, low-stress place. Do not force water, oil, medicine, or feed into the mouth. Contact trained help immediately.

title: Cough or breathing signs in several animals
text: Cough, nasal discharge, fever, or breathing trouble in several animals is a herd/flock warning. Do not mix animals casually, take them to market, or give random antibiotics. Improve ventilation if safe, observe how many are affected, and contact local animal-health support.

title: Restless sounds at night
text: Restlessness or unusual sounds can come from discomfort, environment, feed, heat, injury, or early illness. Check feed/water, dung, belly swelling, breathing, walking, wounds, and whether the animal is lying and rising normally. Escalate if severe restlessness, tight belly, fast breathing, or inability to stand appears.

title: Ear or tail shaking
text: Ear or tail shaking can be a response to flies, irritation, heat, insects, or discomfort. Check wounds, swelling, discharge, ticks, flies, skin marks, and whether appetite and behavior are normal. Escalate if severe pain, wound, pus, fever, or behavior change appears.

title: Licking wall or soil
text: Repeated licking of wall or soil should be observed with feed quality, mineral access, appetite, dung, and behavior. Do not diagnose a deficiency from this sign alone. Check feed changes, salt/mineral access, and whether weakness, poor appetite, weight loss, or abnormal dung is present.

title: Animal down and breathing fast
text: An animal that suddenly falls, is not getting up, or is breathing fast is urgent. Do not force water, feed, oil, or medicine into the mouth. Keep the animal calm and in a safe, ventilated place, check obvious danger around it, and call trained veterinary help immediately.

title: Drooling and not eating
text: Heavy salivation with mouth sores, not eating, fever, or lameness can be a herd-health warning. Do not diagnose exact disease from text alone, but avoid casual mixing or market movement. Contact local animal-health support, especially if more than one animal is affected.

title: Photo or text cannot confirm disease
text: A photo or short text can help identify visible red flags, but it cannot safely confirm disease, medicine, milk safety, or exact treatment. Describe what to check, ask one important follow-up if needed, and escalate when red flags are visible or described.

title: Snake bite or sudden facial swelling
text: Suspected snake bite, especially on the head, muzzle, neck, or leg, is an emergency concern. Watch fast swelling, pain, weakness, collapse, breathing difficulty, bleeding, or nervous signs. Keep calm and avoid running, cutting, sucking, tying tight bands, burning, or applying chemicals. Call trained help quickly.

title: Snake bite myths to block
text: For suspected snake bite, do not advise cutting the bite, sucking venom, tight tourniquets, burning, black-stone treatment, alcohol, kerosene, or herbal antidote claims. These can delay care or worsen injury. Keep the animal quiet, reduce movement, and escalate to trained help.

title: Pesticide or chemical poisoning suspicion
text: Suspected pesticide, insecticide, herbicide, or chemical exposure is urgent, especially with drooling, tremors, diarrhea, breathing trouble, collapse, convulsions, or several animals affected. Remove animals from the source if safe. Do not give home antidotes, oil, alcohol, or human medicines. Preserve label/container information if safe.

title: Suspicious plant or unknown feed poisoning
text: If poisoning is suspected after unknown plants, spoiled feed, sprayed fodder, or unusual feed, stop access to the suspected source and observe how many animals are affected. Red flags include trembling, repeated salivation, severe diarrhea, bloating, collapse, convulsions, or fast breathing. Do not diagnose poison or give antidotes yourself.

title: Electrocution or lightning safety
text: If an animal may have been electrocuted or struck near live wire/lightning, protect people first. Do not touch the animal or wire until power danger is removed. Once safe, keep the area clear and call trained help. Collapse, burns, abnormal breathing, or not getting up after shock is urgent.

title: Severe bleeding or deep wound
text: Heavy bleeding, a deep open wound, horn injury, exposed tissue, or a wound near the eye, abdomen, udder, or joint needs urgent trained help. Keep handling gentle and clean. Do not pack the wound with soil, ash, dung, powder, chili, kerosene, or unknown chemicals. Avoid probing.

title: Fracture or cannot bear weight
text: If a leg looks deformed, the animal cannot bear weight, bone may be broken, or swelling/pain is severe after injury, treat it as urgent. Keep calm and prevent crowding or forced walking. Do not try to set the bone, twist the limb, or tie tight splints without trained guidance.

title: Eye injury or sudden eye problem
text: Eye injury, sudden cloudiness, swelling, bleeding, severe watering, visible wound, or keeping the eye closed needs careful handling and trained help. Do not put chili, ash, human eye drops, oil, or unknown medicine in the eye. Keep away from dust and rough rubbing.

title: Straining to urinate or no urine
text: Repeated straining to urinate, little or no urine, crying, belly pain, swollen belly, or a male goat/sheep unable to pass urine is urgent. Do not give random diuretics, salt, soda, alcohol, or force water. Keep calm and call trained veterinary help quickly.

title: Seizure, tremor, circling, or head pressing
text: Seizures, tremors, circling, head pressing, paralysis, sudden behavior change, or repeated falling are urgent signs. Causes can include poisoning, infection, injury, metabolic illness, or other serious problems. Do not diagnose from text, restrain roughly, force liquids, or give medicines yourself. Keep people safe and call trained help.

title: Sudden death and carcass handling boundary
text: Sudden unexplained death, especially with bleeding from body openings, no stiffness, or multiple deaths, can be a public-health warning. Do not cut open, butcher, skin, eat, sell, or move the carcass casually. Keep people and animals away and contact official animal-health authorities or a veterinarian.

title: Do not open suspected disease carcass
text: Do not open, cut, skin, or process an animal that died suddenly or may have infectious disease. Some carcass handling can expose people and contaminate the area. Do not give meat or milk safety clearance from text. Keep children, dogs, and other animals away and seek official guidance.

title: Heat stroke emergency signs
text: Heat stress becomes urgent when the animal is panting or breathing very fast, drooling, weak, staggering, collapsed, not drinking, or several animals are affected during hot humid weather. Provide shade/airflow and clean water access if safe. Do not force water into a weak or collapsed animal. Get trained help quickly.

title: Choking or blocked throat suspicion
text: If feed seems stuck in the throat, the animal cannot swallow, coughs repeatedly while eating, has froth/saliva, distress, or belly swelling, treat it as urgent. Do not push sticks, pipes, bottles, oil, or hands deep into the mouth/throat. Keep calm and call trained help.

title: Chemical burn or caustic exposure
text: Chemical exposure, acid/caustic contact, kerosene/phenyl application, or burn-like skin injury needs careful safety handling. Keep people safe and avoid adding more chemicals or irritants. Do not neutralize with random substances. Move away from the source if safe and seek trained help, especially near eyes, mouth, udder, or deep injury.

title: No meat or milk clearance in dangerous disease situations
text: Do not give meat or milk safety clearance when there is sudden death, suspected infectious disease, abnormal milk, poisoning, bite/saliva exposure, or multiple sick animals. Keep products separate, avoid sale/use, and seek official veterinary or public-health guidance.

title: Short rural helper style
text: For farmer-facing answers, keep language short, practical, and close to the user's language. Prefer one safe first step, one or two checks, and one escalation condition. Avoid long legal disclaimers, unrelated warnings, and generic policy spam.
